payload.answer: Store durable memory as compact metadata/policy (schema and retrieval strategy), and keep the full 500-row CSV as an external artifact or queryable source with an index/pointer for on-demand row retrieval.
payload.decision: Do not store the full 500-row table as durable memory cards; instead store only a compact schema/pattern and, if needed, a retrieval pointer or aggregated statistics/index, while keeping row-level data in a separate external source that can be queried on demand.

entity_resolution.reason: No specific real-world entities beyond generic table columns are provided; the content is a structural/storage decision rather than entity extraction.

payload.intent: Decide how to store a large CSV table (500 rows, repeated id/name/value) in Brain’s personal memory system.

memory_cards.statement: For a large dataset context (e.g., CSV with ~500 rows and repeated id/name/value fields), store only compact metadata (schema, column meanings, and any aggregation/index needed for retrieval) in Brain as durable memory; avoid persisting every row as individual memory cards.

If future queries require specific rows, keep the raw table outside Brain (or as an external artifact) and store a retrieval pointer plus a deterministic query/index strategy (e.g., lookup by id, then name/value), rather than duplicating row contents as memory.

details: Committed a general storage policy and retrieval guideline suitable for large repeated-row CSV contexts.
Avoided committing any specific row values (not provided) and avoided duplicating the full table into durable memory cards.

repair_options: If row-level facts must be retained, compress into summaries per id (or per name) and store only aggregated/validated representations rather than all repeated rows.
Create a small set of representative examples (e.g., a few id/name/value rows) as non-exhaustive memory, and rely on external data for exact matches.
Store a hash/checksum and version tag for the CSV artifact to ensure future retrieval consistency without duplicating all contents.
